description: A drawing or diagram showing the spatial relationships of different physical features in the game world. A map is divided into named regions, which may contain areas, steadings, and/or sites.

A large swath of landscape with a set boundary. Its overall character is determined by its dominant natural or political aspect—such as a terrain type (forest, desert) or a political border (kingdom, barony). Regions break the map into distinct, manageable units.

A written record of a region's contents, including tags, places, general impressions, obstacles, hazards, and anything else of interest. An almanac is a living document that can grow and change over the course of play.
A smaller piece of geography within a region that stands out from the region's overall character in some notable way—such as a hunting ground within a great forest, or an island in a sea.
A village, town, city, or other settlement.
A point of interest within a region, area, or steading—such as a waterfall in a jungle, a monastery in the mountains, or a chieftain's hut in a village.
A collective term for regions, areas, steadings, and sites.
Anything the party finds that's interesting but not an immediate threat. It might be beneficial (a freshwater spring), worth investigating (an overgrown ruin), or an obstacle (a bog or chasm).

Anything which, if left unchecked, may be harmful. Traps and hostile creatures are the most common types. A Danger encountered during a journey may be incidental, or may become part of a larger Front at the GM's discretion.
